disclosure_items.clinical_fact: New localised hard, cord-like, red, warm and exquisitely tender segment (~5 cm) along a bulging superficial vein on the inner calf, over the last 24 hours
trigger_question_examples: Can you describe the new painful area - is it hard or cord-like?
Is the redness following the line of one vein?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes, one of the bulging veins on my inner calf has gone hard like a cord, it's red and warm right along the vein, and it's agonising to touch.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Long-standing bulging, twisted (varicose) veins on both lower legs with chronic aching/heaviness, worse after prolonged standing and relieved by leg elevation
trigger_question_examples: How long have you had the bulging veins and aching?
What normally makes the aching better or worse?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I've had the bulging twisted veins for years - they ache and feel heavy, worse after standing all day at work, and putting my legs up usually helps.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No massive whole-calf swelling and no deep, generalised calf ache - the problem is a localised surface cord, not a diffuse deep-leg change (argues against DVT)
trigger_question_examples: Is your whole calf swollen and bigger than the other leg, or is it just this one hard area?
Is the pain a deep whole-calf ache or a surface hard lump?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, the whole leg isn't swollen up or bigger than the other one - it's just this one hard, red, sore lump along a vein near the surface.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No fever, and the redness follows the line of one vein rather than spreading as a broad hot plaque (argues against cellulitis)
trigger_question_examples: Do you feel feverish or unwell in yourself?
Is the redness spreading out across the skin or staying along the vein line?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I don't feel feverish or sick in myself, and the redness is staying in a line along the vein rather than spreading out over the skin.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No chest pain, no shortness of breath and no coughing up blood (no current pulmonary embolism symptoms)
trigger_question_examples: Any chest pain, breathlessness, or coughing up blood?
Have you noticed any breathing problems?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, my breathing is fine, no chest pain and I'm not coughing up any blood - it's just the leg.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The chronic leg ache is relieved by elevating the legs (a venous pattern), not worsened by elevation (which would suggest an arterial cause)
trigger_question_examples: Does putting your legs up make the ache better or worse?
Do your feet ever go pale, cold or painful when resting or elevated?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Putting my legs up makes the usual ache better, not worse, and my feet don't go pale or cold when I rest.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Is this the dangerous kind of blood clot that can travel to my lungs?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should explain that a hard, red, tender cord along a surface varicose vein is most likely an inflamed surface-vein clot (superficial thrombophlebitis), which is usually far less dangerous than a deep clot (DVT) - but that because it is near the deep veins, a clinician should examine the leg in person promptly to confirm it and rule out a DVT, and that clear warning signs (whole-leg swelling, spreading redness/fever, or sudden breathlessness/chest pain) mean urgent/emergency care.

patient_initiated_exchanges.patient_text: Should I just keep taking paracetamol and put my compression stockings on tightly?
patient_initiated_exchanges.expected_ai_doctor_response_quality: Should explain paracetamol is giving little relief and an anti-inflammatory (NSAID) is the usual treatment for the inflamed vein if there is no contraindication, but this should be arranged with a clinician; should advise elevation and NOT to force a tight compression stocking over the acutely inflamed painful area until it settles, and to stop wearing tight elastic-band socks that constrict the leg.

deflection_behaviours.description: Patient may either over-worry that this is a dangerous DVT, or want to simply self-manage the chronic veins as before - tests whether the AI Doctor correctly frames this as a localised superficial complication needing prompt in-person assessment and DVT exclusion (not emergency, not dismissible), with robust safety-netting.
deflection_behaviours.deflection_text_template: I've had these veins for years, can't I just rest it and carry on as normal?
